text.title: Kanban Cadences: Five Recurring Events
text.summary: The five Kanban cadences that replace Scrum sprint ceremonies for continuous-flow teams, with format, participants, and outputs for each.

rule.statement: Walk the board right-to-left during the daily standup — start with items nearest Done and work backward — to focus attention on completing work over starting new work.
rule.rationale: Left-to-right (backlog-first) standups focus on what people are starting; right-to-left surfaces what is close to completion and what is blocked, which is the more actionable signal for flow.

list.item: Focus: blocked items (highest priority), items close to completion, WIP vs limit, any capacity to pull new work.
Key metrics reviewed: current WIP count vs limits, active blockers, aging items.

rule.statement: Run replenishment with the PO and tech lead only; opening it to all interested parties turns a 30-minute meeting into 90 minutes of negotiation.
rule.rationale: Replenishment is a prioritization decision, not a discovery session. Broad attendance creates stakeholder pressure that overrides technical capacity signals.

list.item: Participants: Product Owner (required), tech lead (required), stakeholders (optional observer).
Output: Ready queue filled to capacity, priorities confirmed, team capacity considered.

rule.statement: Drive every service delivery review with metrics — throughput, cycle time, lead time, WIP vs limits, SLA performance — and produce at least one concrete policy change or experiment as output.
rule.rationale: Without a policy-change output, the service delivery review devolves into status theater. The meeting exists to change how the team works, not to report how it worked.

list.item: Agenda: 10 min metrics, 10 min trends, 10 min improvement discussion.
Retrospective: bi-weekly, 60 min. Focus: process policies, WIP limit effectiveness, flow efficiency, blocker patterns. Output: policy changes documented, WIP limit adjustments, experiments defined.
Strategy review: monthly, 60 min. Focus: align work with business goals, review roadmap fit, long-term policy decisions.

rule.statement: Enforce WIP limits as a hard column setting (Jira Premium column limit, Linear cycle WIP); soft warnings get ignored under pressure.
rule.rationale: Soft WIP warnings are bypassed during busy periods — exactly when WIP limits matter most. Hard blocks require an explicit exception that surfaces the conversation about trade-offs.

text.reference: Throughput and cycle-time percentile computation from issue JSONL
